text: The surgeon performs a sigmoid resection for a colovesical fistula, encountering significant scarring in the left retroperitoneum. The colon is adhered to the bladder, and the surgeon carefully dissects to restore normal anatomy, focusing on the colon to avoid damaging the bladder. A small intramural abscess is visible at the fistula site.

Nothing glamorous about these cases, just stay focused, don't lose your anatomy, and don't work in deep holes that are filled with blood. Just keep moving around, good use of suction to clear the blood and smoke, and there's a blunt dissector, and just keep slowly working. Trying to work down the right pelvis. Didn't really like what I was seeing, so come back over to the left. The tensile strength of the robotic instruments I do feel has an advantage over that for straight stick laparoscopy. Simply for the reduction in the tension of the instruments in your hands, which can be very taxing and straining and uncomfortable over the course of a long surgery with tissue like this. Nonetheless, these cases are absolutely done doable laparoscopically. And I don't think that the simple development of a fistula or the knowledge that a case would look like this should sway somebody away from doing laparoscopic surgery. I think that these patients tend to benefit the most for minimally invasive surgery.

The surgeon maintains meticulous hemostasis while observing that the colon is extensively adhered to the bladder. The surgical field does not reveal the pelvis, prompting the surgeon to navigate back over the pelvic brim. The common iliac artery is visible to the left, and a small window is found to access the pelvis. The surgeon carefully dissects within the scarred plane between the colon and the left pelvic sidewall, using blunt dissection to open the area anterior to the sigmoid colon, despite the fusion of tissues.

The surgeon accesses the right pelvis and works to detach the colon from the back of the bladder, revealing pus from an intramural abscess. The surgeon performs lateral mobilization, noting the absence of retroperitoneal fat due to a previous nephrectomy, and checks the length for a tension-free anastomosis. The mesentery is taken from proximal to distal, with a low ligation close to the bowel wall, marking the distal margin for orientation.

The surgeon uses a stapler to secure the distal end of the colon after taking the mesentery at the proximal and distal margins. The camera view is adjusted from port three to two, with the staple port placed through the right rectus. The surgeon lines up the anastomosis, checking perfusion to ensure the lip enhances green, indicating adequate blood supply. Sutures are placed on one side, then from the middle, and finally on the other side, completing the anastomosis.

The surgeon completes a full two-layer anastomosis using 2-0 PDS sutures, taking seromuscular bites around the anastomosis and tying it to the original posterior suture. A leak test is performed on the colorectal anastomosis using methylene blue, which shows no leakage. The bladder is also tested with methylene blue and saline, inflated to 400 cc's, revealing no leaks despite the presence of a small intramural abscess. The decision is made not to leave a drain, and the Foley catheter is planned to be removed after forty-eight hours.
